findings.ref: human-copy/sdd-hook-guard.py _shell_cwd_write_hits_protected (initial current_dir='', cwd_known=True); same in .js shellCwdWriteHitsProtected
findings.note: Residual cross-call variant remains: the per-call analysis assumes each command starts at the project root, so under a persistent shell 'cd <protected-dir>' in one tool call followed by 'rm <basename>' in a later call is still allowed. This is outside REQ-002's stated scope (tracking transitions across compound-command segments within one command), hence non-blocking; consider anchoring to the hook payload cwd field or starting with cwd_known=False as future hardening.

AC-005 evidence in the bundle covers the read-only short-circuit and a mirrored subset of the existing guard-parity shell corpus (scenarios 17,18,23-29) with per-case .py/.js parity assertions; a full run of the existing protected suites against the applied twins is deferred to the independent quality gate per the pre-gate semantics note. Corpus nits: no append-redirect-after-cd (>>) case and no explicit popd-then-write case.

findings.ref: artifact sanitization ([REDACTED]/[PATH_REDACTED] placeholders in both guard twins)
findings.note: The sanitizer redacted several load-bearing constants (_SHELL_REDIRECT_TOKEN_RE, _SHELL_UNSAFE_TOKEN_CHARS, tokenizer initializers, approval regexes), so their exact patterns could not be statically verified from the bundle; their behavior is verified only indirectly via the recorded RED/GREEN corpus, and the staged-file hashes cannot be recomputed from the sanitized text (internal MANIFEST/header consistency was checked instead).
